topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with a low, irritating buzz, each clink of ceramic and murmur of conversation grating on my nerves. I stared at my phone, the screen mocking me with the unread messages from Liam. Another hour. Another polite deflection. I tapped my foot against the worn floorboards, a restless energy thrumming beneath my skin. I needed an answer, a confirmation that the shared history we’d built wasn't just a fragile construct in my own head. When Liam finally arrived, his smile was easy, too easy, and it didn't reach his eyes. "Hey," he said, sliding into the seat opposite me. I leaned forward, my knuckles white where I gripped my mug. "So, about last summer? Remember that trip to the coast?" I waited, my gaze locked on him, hoping for a flicker of recognition, a shared memory sparking between us. He blinked slowly, a slight pause stretching the silence until it became unbearable. "Uh, I don't think so," he admitted, shifting uncomfortably. The air in my lungs seemed to seize up.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my small apartment three times before stopping abruptly, running a hand through my already messy hair. The clock on the wall seemed to mock my stillness, ticking away the seconds I felt I was wasting. I had rehearsed this conversation a hundred times in my head, finding the words smooth and certain, yet the moment I opened my mouth, they dissolved into a dry, useless rasp. Liam was sitting on the sofa, scrolling through his tablet, radiating an air of complete disinterest. "Look," I started, my voice tight, trying to inject some urgency into the mundane topic. "We used to spend every summer building forts in the woods behind my house. You remember that, right?" I watched his thumb stop moving on the screen. He looked up, a faint, distant look in his eyes, as if I were a character in a very old, unfamiliar movie. "I... I don't recall," he murmured, a shrug accompanying the statement. A sharp, hot sensation rose in my chest, making my breath catch. I felt the need to push, to demand that he *should* remember, but the words wouldn't form.

[story 3]

The silence between us was thick, heavy, pressing down on my eardrums. I kept checking my watch, the movement of the second hand a frantic, visible reminder of how much time was slipping away. I wanted a reaction, anything to break the suffocating quiet. I leaned across the table, my expression carefully neutral, trying to mask the churning frustration inside. "Seriously, Liam, what's the deal?" I asked, the edge in my tone sharper than intended. "We were inseparable. Everything we did—you know it. Don't you remember anything?" He looked genuinely puzzled, tilting his head slightly. "I feel like I should, but nothing sticks," he confessed, running a hand over his jaw. The impatience coiled tight in my gut, a physical tension that made my jaw clench. I wanted him to just *get it*, to acknowledge the obvious thread connecting us, but he seemed utterly adrift, leaving me stranded on the shore of his forgetfulness.
